creativeNotes: This is strong, thoughtful work with real craft in the internal-voice writing and branch architecture — but there's a structural bug at the heart of it that has to be fixed before this ships: the player character and the NPC 'Maya' share the same name, and the script leans into it as a cute bit ('I'm Maya, also Maya') without ever resolving the confusion it creates. By scene-7, when Ms. Alvarez pairs 'Maya Ortiz-Chen and Jordan Kessler,' a player who chose the confident-table path will have spent three scenes with an NPC named Maya and will now reasonably wonder whose name is on the board. This isn't a nitpick — it undermines clarity in a branching narrative where players need to track who's who without narration doing extra work. Beyond that, the writing is genuinely good: the self-interrupting internal monologue ('I've done this before. / (I have never done this before.)') is one of the most authentic teen-anxiety voices I've seen in this pipeline, and the Jordan/bird-book reveal in scene-9a is a legitimately earned emotional beat, not a manufactured one.

story: The name collision between player and NPC Maya (scenes 3a, 4, 5c, and referenced again in scene-7's pairing) is a real continuity problem, not just a joke — fix by renaming the NPC or giving the player a different default name in this world.

The 'Popular Table' branch (Maya-NPC storyline) simply evaporates after scene-6a — there's no acknowledgment in scene-7 or beyond of what happened with that friendship, even though the whole episode is otherwise obsessive about consequences carrying forward. If Jordan's arc gets four converging endings, Maya's storyline deserves at least a single line of closure or callback.
Structurally the six-scene convergence into the shared project-partner spine is well designed and mirrors the emotionalArc excellently — pacing from anxious arrival to vulnerable disclosure lands.

Consider letting at least one choice have a visible school-wide consequence beyond the immediate scene — right now every choice pays off socially/emotionally but never structurally (e.g., seating never affects who else notices you later).
character: Jordan is the standout — the shield-of-a-book detail, the 'emergency-bathroom-route memorized' line, and the grandmother's bird book reveal are specific, unforced, and true to a socially anxious transfer student. This character earns the episode's climax.

Tyler and Chloe are functional but thin — Tyler's 'Tour costs five bucks' and 'Bet the book doesn't even talk back' land as generic bully-lite dialogue. Chloe's recurring 'half-second late laugh' beat, however, is a genuinely sharp piece of characterization — she's clearly performing loyalty she doesn't fully feel. Lean harder into Chloe as the more interesting character of the two; Tyler needs one more specific, non-generic line to avoid feeling like a stock antagonist.
Maya (NPC) has a strong, warm introduction (scene-3a) but disappears from meaningful narrative function after the lunch choice — she deserves at least a closing beat if the player chose her table.
dialogue: The internal monologue voice is excellent throughout — self-corrections, hedges, and parentheticals ('Nobody's looking at you. Probably.') feel authentically 12-14-year-old without being cutesy.
Ms. Alvarez's 'Yes, I know exactly what I'm doing' has real teacher-authority voice — one of the strongest adult-character lines in the episode.
Jordan's dry deflections ('The full-body deer-in-headlights thing? Yeah, kind of a giveaway') read as genuinely funny and specific rather than writer's-room-quippy.

A few Tyler lines ('Whatever, it's not that deep') repeat almost verbatim in two different branches (choice-3 response and scene-6a2) — vary the phrasing so repeat playthroughs don't feel like reused stock dialogue.
tone: The tonal balance between cafeteria-social-comedy and quiet vulnerability is well calibrated — the episode never tips into after-school-special territory even in its most earnest beat (scene-9a).
The emotionalArc document is honored almost beat-for-beat in the actual scene writing, which is a good sign of intentional design rather than improvisation.

The 'birds feel like the closest thing I've got' reveal is earned specifically because it's understated and not over-explained — protect that restraint in future episodes; don't let Jordan over-narrate the metaphor.

inspirationReferences.title: PEN15
inspirationReferences.reasoning: The internal-monologue-vs-external-performance gap and the specific cringe-but-true details of new-kid anxiety match this episode's best writing instincts.

inspirationReferences.title: Life is Strange
inspirationReferences.reasoning: The branching cafeteria/social-choice structure and the way small kindness-or-cruelty choices ripple into a semester-long relationship mirror this game's approach to earned consequence over spectacle.

inspirationReferences.title: Wonder by R.J. Palacio
inspirationReferences.reasoning: Jordan's arc — an outsider whose interiority is only revealed through patient, unforced connection — echoes this book's insistence that empathy is a series of small choices, not one big gesture.
